reasoning: The exact base value on 2025-10-26 (a Sunday) will likely default to the last available rate from Oct 24 (which is 3.94%) or the Monday Oct 27 rate, both of which precede the Oct 28-29 FOMC meeting. The market (CME FedWatch) prices in a 99% probability of a 25 basis point rate cut at this meeting, meaning the effective federal funds rate—and consequently the 4-week T-bill yield—will drop mechanically immediately afterward. Therefore, by the first resolution date (Nov 2), the yield will fully incorporate the lower post-meeting rate (likely around 3.80%-3.85%), making an increase from the ~3.94% pre-meeting baseline highly improbable. By the second resolution date (Nov 25), the 4-week bill will begin pricing in yet another 25 bps cut widely expected at the December FOMC meeting, driving the yield even lower. Finally, by Jan 24, 2026, additional easing or a stabilized lower rate environment is expected to keep yields below the late October 2025 baseline. However, as per the instruction to widen uncertainties over longer time horizons, the probability of an unexpected increase (e.g., from an inflation spike or a sudden Fed pivot) rises for distant dates. Hence, the probabilities for an increase are scaled upwards over time to reflect this rising risk variance, remaining well below 0.50 due to the strong expected downward trajectory of short-term rates.
